walkthrough.title: Place the Island Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Isle of Cats set up. Place the island board in the center of the table. This board represents the mysterious isle where the cats live. It has a fish track around the edge and fields where cat tiles will be placed each round.

walkthrough.title: Set the Day Tracker
walkthrough.text: Place the day tracker on Day 1. The game lasts five days, which are your five rounds. Place the Vesh Darkhand figure on the day track to mark the approaching villain. Each day the threat gets closer, so every round matters.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Island with Cats
walkthrough.text: Draw cat tiles from the bag and place them in the fields on the island board. The number of cats depends on the player count and round. Cat tiles are polyomino shapes in five colors: blue, green, red, purple, and orange. These are the cats you will be rescuing to your boat.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Supply
walkthrough.text: Place fish tokens and boot tokens in supply piles near the board. Fish are the game's currency. You will spend fish to keep cards and rescue cats. Boot tokens determine turn order when rescuing.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Discovery Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all one hundred and fifty discovery cards into a single deck. This deck contains rescue cards, lesson cards, treasure cards, and Oshax cards. You will draft from this deck each round. Place treasure tiles in their supply areas as well.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Boat Boards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a boat board. This is your personal grid where you will place rescued cat tiles. Your boat has seven rooms divided by walls. At the end of the game, each room that is not completely filled with tiles costs you five points. Filling rooms is critical.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player starts with twenty fish. This is your entire budget for the game, although you will earn more fish each round. Deal seven discovery cards to each player as well. Players also receive starting boot tokens for turn order.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Boat Grid
walkthrough.text: Take a close look at your boat board. The grid has seven rooms separated by interior walls. Some spaces have rat symbols printed on them. Uncovered rats cost you one point each at game end. Your goal is to cover as much of this grid as possible with cat tiles and treasures.

walkthrough.title: Explore the Cat Tiles
walkthrough.text: Cat tiles come in polyomino shapes of one to five squares. They look like Tetris pieces. Larger cats cover more space but are harder to fit. Smaller cats are easier to place but cover less. You will need to balance big and small cats to fill your boat efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Choose a random first player. The game plays over five rounds, each representing one day. Each day you will fish for income, draft cards, play lesson cards, rescue cats, and discover rare treasures. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: In Isle of Cats, you are rescuing cats from a mysterious island before the villain Vesh Darkhand arrives. Cats come as polyomino tiles that you must fit onto your boat's grid. Over five rounds, you draft cards, spend fish to rescue cats, and arrange them on your boat. The player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: The Five Phases
walkthrough.text: Each round has five phases in order: Fishing, Explore, Read Lessons, Rescue Cats, and Rare Finds. Every player completes each phase before moving to the next. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Fishing
walkthrough.text: All players gain fish tokens based on the current day. Fish are the currency of Isle of Cats. You spend fish to keep cards during the draft and to rescue cats from the island. Manage your fish carefully because running out means fewer options.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Explore (Card Draft)
walkthrough.text: Deal seven cards to each player. You draft cards using a two-at-a-time method. Choose two cards from your hand to keep, then pass the remaining five to the next player. Pick two more from the new hand of five, pass three, and continue until all cards are drafted. Here is the catch: each card has a fish cost printed on it. You must pay the cost immediately or discard the card.

walkthrough.title: Types of Cards
walkthrough.text: There are several types of discovery cards. Rescue cards come in pairs and determine which color cat you can rescue and your turn order priority. Lesson cards create scoring objectives. Treasure cards grant access to treasure tiles. Oshax cards let you claim wild cat tiles that count as any color.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Read Lessons
walkthrough.text: Players reveal any public lesson cards they drafted this round. Public lessons create shared scoring objectives that all players can work toward, like three points per row with three or more cats. Private lesson cards stay hidden and score based on your secret objectives at game end.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: Rescue Cats
walkthrough.text: This is the main event. In turn order, players take turns rescuing cat tiles from the island. To rescue a cat, play a pair of rescue cards from your hand. The cards determine the color of cat you can take and your speed priority. Select a matching cat tile from the island and place it on your boat grid.

walkthrough.title: Placing Cat Tiles
walkthrough.text: When you place a cat tile on your boat, it must fit entirely within the grid and cannot overlap other tiles already placed. Cat tiles are polyomino shapes of varying sizes, from one square up to five squares. Larger cats cover more room space but are much harder to fit. Think of it like a jigsaw puzzle that you build piece by piece.

walkthrough.title: Speed and Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Rescue cards have speed values. Higher speed means you pick your cat from the island first. Going first gives you the best selection of shapes and colors. Draft high-speed rescue cards when you need a specific cat tile that others might also want.

walkthrough.title: Permanent Baskets
walkthrough.text: Some cards grant permanent baskets. Normally you need a pair of rescue cards to take one cat. Permanent baskets persist between rounds, letting you rescue cats without needing rescue card pairs. These are very valuable because they provide free rescues every round for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Phase Five: Rare Finds
walkthrough.text: Certain cards grant access to rare treasures. These are small tiles, usually one or two squares, that you can place on your boat to fill gaps. Rare treasures are worth victory points and help you cover those awkward empty spaces that cat tiles cannot fill.

walkthrough.title: Cat Families
walkthrough.text: A family is a group of three or more adjacent same-colored cats on your boat. Adjacent means orthogonally connected, not diagonal. Families score bonus points based on size: three cats score eight points, four cats score eleven, five cats score fifteen, and six or more score twenty points.

walkthrough.title: Oshax Wild Cats
walkthrough.text: Oshax tiles are wild cats. When scoring families, an Oshax adjacent to a color group counts as that color. If an Oshax is adjacent to multiple colors, you choose which family it joins. Oshax tiles are extremely valuable for bridging gaps between color groups or extending a family to a higher scoring tier.

walkthrough.title: Rooms and Penalties
walkthrough.text: Your boat has seven rooms separated by walls. At game end, every room that is not completely filled with tiles costs you five points. With seven rooms, that is a potential thirty-five point penalty if you leave rooms empty. Additionally, any uncovered rat symbols on the boat cost one point each. Filling your boat is essential.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Winning
walkthrough.text: After five rounds, calculate your final score. Add points for cat families based on their sizes. Add points for rare treasures. Add points for public and private lesson cards you have met. Then subtract five points for each unfilled room and one point for each uncovered rat. The player with the highest total wins.

bullets: Rescue cats (polyomino tiles) from island to your boat grid
5 rounds; draft cards, spend fish, place tiles
Highest total score wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome Aboard
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Isle of Cats! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first two rounds of a game with three example players. You will see how drafting, rescuing, and tile placement work. Follow along and you will be ready to fill your boat with cats.

walkthrough.title: Day 1: Fishing Phase
walkthrough.text: All players receive their fish income for Day 1. Each player starts with twenty fish from setup and now gains additional fish for the round. Fish are your only currency, used for keeping drafted cards and interacting with the island. Guard them carefully.

walkthrough.title: Day 1: The Draft Begins
walkthrough.text: Seven cards are dealt to each player. Player 1 looks through their hand and sees two rescue cards, a private lesson card, a treasure card, and three other cards. They must choose two to keep. Each card has a fish cost, so they need to balance what they want versus what they can afford.

walkthrough.title: Drafting Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player 1 keeps a private lesson card that rewards having cats of all five colors and a blue rescue card. The lesson card costs three fish and the rescue card costs one fish. That is four fish spent, leaving plenty for the rest of the draft. They pass the remaining five cards to the next player.

walkthrough.title: Completing the Draft
walkthrough.text: All players continue drafting two cards at a time, paying fish for each card they keep. By the end of the draft, each player has seven cards and a smaller fish supply. The key is having the right mix of rescue cards, lesson cards, and possibly treasure or Oshax cards.

walkthrough.title: Lessons Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 2 reveals a public lesson card: three points for every row on your boat that contains three or more cats. All players can now work toward this shared objective. Player 1 keeps their private lesson hidden. Public lessons change the strategic landscape for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Rescue Phase: First Cat
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has the highest-speed rescue card pair and goes first. They play two green rescue cards and select a large five-square green cat from the island. They carefully place it on their boat, covering five squares in one of the rooms. Great coverage, but that is a big shape to fit.

walkthrough.title: Placing the Cat on the Boat
walkthrough.text: When Player 3 places the green cat tile, they think about future placements. They put it near the edge of a room, leaving the center area open for smaller cats to fill in later. Good spatial planning makes future rounds much easier.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Bad Tile Placement
walkthrough.text: Player 1 rescues a blue cat tile and places it right in the center of a room, creating small awkward gaps on all four sides. These gaps are too small for most cat tiles but not easily filled by treasures either. Player 1 has created dead space that will be very hard to fill.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Place from the Edges In
walkthrough.text: Always start placing cat tiles from the edges and corners of your boat, working inward. When you place in the center first, you create odd-shaped gaps around all sides that are nearly impossible to fill. Edge-first placement ensures you always have a large open center area that can accept any shape.

walkthrough.title: Day 2: More Cats, Harder Choices
walkthrough.text: Day 2 begins with fishing and a new draft. The island is refilled with fresh cat tiles. Players now have some tiles on their boats and can see what shapes and colors they need. The draft decisions get more interesting because you are building on what you started yesterday.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Room Coverage
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been drafting all lesson cards and rescue cards for blue cats, hoping to build one massive blue family. But they have only covered two of their seven rooms. With three rounds left, they risk losing twenty-five points from unfilled rooms. A twenty-point blue family does not make up for that penalty.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Room Coverage Before Big Families
walkthrough.text: Filling all seven rooms should be your first priority. Each unfilled room costs you five points, which adds up devastatingly fast. A boat with all rooms filled and modest families will almost always outscore a boat with huge families but empty rooms. Plan your tile placements to cover rooms first, then optimize for color families within that constraint.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Spreading Colors Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has been rescuing one cat of each color, hoping for variety. But with only one or two cats of each color, none of them form families. Families need three or more adjacent same-colored cats to score. Player 3 has five individual cats and zero family bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Focus on Two or Three Colors
walkthrough.text: Instead of spreading across all five colors, focus on two or three. This gives you a realistic chance of building families of three or more cats. A single family of five cats scores fifteen points. Five individual cats of different colors score zero family points. Focus your rescue cards on the colors you are already building.

walkthrough.title: Smart Placement: Using Oshax
walkthrough.text: Player 1 drafted an Oshax card and claims a wild cat tile. They place it between a group of two blue cats and two green cats. The Oshax counts as blue, completing a three-cat blue family for eight points. Oshax tiles are incredibly flexible and can bridge two groups or push a family to a higher tier.

walkthrough.title: Treasures Fill the Gaps
walkthrough.text: Player 2 uses treasure cards to claim small one-square and two-square treasure tiles. These fit perfectly in the awkward gaps between cat tiles. Treasures are not part of families, but they cover room space and rat symbols. Never underestimate the value of small tiles for filling gaps.

walkthrough.title: Continue the Game
walkthrough.text: The game continues for three more days. Each round follows the same five phases. As your boat fills up, placement decisions become more critical because space is limited. Keep drafting rescue cards for your focus colors, fill rooms whenever possible, and use treasures and Oshax to optimize your score.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through your first practice session of Isle of Cats. You have seen how drafting, rescuing, and placement work, and you have learned from three common beginner mistakes: placing tiles in the center instead of from the edges, ignoring room coverage for big families, and spreading colors too thin. You are ready to rescue cats.

walkthrough.title: Before You Set Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on grid management and color focus.

Second, reference the Appendix for scoring details and card types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest beginner mistakes in Isle of Cats. First, placing tiles in the center of the boat and creating unfillable gaps. Second, chasing big families while leaving rooms empty. Third, spreading across all five colors instead of focusing on two or three. Fourth, spending too much fish on expensive lesson cards and running out during rescues. Keep these in mind from the start.

walkthrough.title: Fill Your Rooms First
walkthrough.text: Each of your boat's seven rooms that is not completely filled at game end costs you five points. That is the most punishing penalty in the game. Before worrying about families, colors, or lessons, make sure every room is completely covered with tiles. A player who fills all seven rooms with random cats will almost always outscore a player with amazing families but three empty rooms.

walkthrough.title: Place from Edges Inward
walkthrough.text: When placing cat tiles on your boat, start from the edges and corners of each room and work toward the center. This keeps a large open area in the middle where you can fit any shape. If you place tiles in the center first, you create odd gaps around the edges that only very specific shapes can fill. The edge-inward approach gives you maximum flexibility throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Two or Three Colors
walkthrough.text: Cat families score based on groups of three or more adjacent same-colored cats. Spreading across all five colors means you rarely get groups large enough to score. Instead, pick two or three colors early and draft rescue cards that match those colors. A family of five scores fifteen points. Five individual cats of different colors score zero family points. Color focus is the path to big points.

walkthrough.title: Balance Fish Spending
walkthrough.text: Fish are limited, and you need them for both keeping drafted cards and rescuing cats. New players often spend too much on expensive lesson cards during the draft and then cannot afford to rescue enough cats. A good rule of thumb is to keep your fish spending balanced. Two to three lesson cards per game is usually enough. Prioritize rescue cards over expensive lessons.

walkthrough.title: Small Tiles Are Valuable
walkthrough.text: Small cat tiles of one or two squares may not look exciting, but they are incredibly valuable for filling gaps. Large five-square cats cover a lot of space but are rigid in shape. Small cats fit anywhere. Draft some treasure cards and look for small cat tiles on the island. They are the glue that holds your boat together and prevents room penalties.

walkthrough.title: Oshax Are Premium
walkthrough.text: Oshax wild cats are among the most valuable tiles in the game. They count as any color for family scoring, meaning they can bridge two groups or extend a family to a higher scoring tier. When you see an Oshax card in the draft, strongly consider taking it. A well-placed Oshax can easily be worth eight to ten points by connecting color groups that would otherwise score nothing.

walkthrough.title: Plan for Lessons Early
walkthrough.text: Private lesson cards can be worth ten or more points each, making them some of the highest-scoring cards in the game. But they only score if you meet their conditions. Draft lessons that align with what you are already building. If you are focusing on blue and green cats, a lesson that rewards having three colors is easy to meet. A lesson that requires all five colors might be impossible. Choose lessons that match your natural strategy.

bullets: Don't place tiles center-first — creates unfillable gaps
Don't chase families while rooms are empty
Don't spread across all 5 colors — focus on 2-3
Don't overspend fish on expensive lesson cards

walkthrough.title: Grid Optimization Theory
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their entire boat layout before placing a single tile. Visualize which rooms will use large cat tiles for coverage and which rooms will be filled with small tiles and treasures. The most efficient boats use large tiles in the middle rooms and small tiles to fill edges and corners. Having a mental blueprint reduces wasted space dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Draft Economy
walkthrough.text: Fish management in the draft is the hidden skill of Isle of Cats. Advanced players spend the minimum fish needed per draft round. Rescue cards are cheap and essential. Lesson cards are expensive but can be worth it if they align with your plan. The key is never drafting a card you cannot afford to use. A drafted lesson card that costs fish you need for rescues is worse than useless because it consumes budget and never scores.

walkthrough.title: Permanent Basket Priority
walkthrough.text: Permanent basket cards are the most efficient cards in the game. A permanent basket drafted in round one provides a free rescue in rounds two through five. That is four free cats from a single card. Draft permanent baskets as early as possible to maximize the number of rounds they are active. In the later rounds, their value drops because they have fewer rounds to trigger.

walkthrough.title: Family Size Optimization
walkthrough.text: Family scoring has tiers: three cats for eight points, four for eleven, five for fifteen, and six or more for twenty. The jump from three to four is only three points per cat, but the jump from five to six or more is five points for one extra cat. The most efficient family size is three cats for eight points, giving you the best points per tile. But if you can build two large families of five or more, the scoring becomes excellent.

walkthrough.title: Oshax Placement Theory
walkthrough.text: Oshax placement is the highest-leverage decision in Isle of Cats. An Oshax placed between two same-colored groups can merge them into one large family, potentially jumping from two groups of two scoring zero to one family of five scoring fifteen. The ideal Oshax placement maximizes adjacency to your focus colors. Position your regular cats to leave Oshax-shaped gaps between color groups.

walkthrough.title: Lesson Card Stacking
walkthrough.text: The most powerful scoring comes from stacking multiple lesson cards that reward the same behavior. If two public lessons and one private lesson all reward placing cats in rows, then every row you fill scores triple. Draft lesson cards that overlap with public lessons already in play. This creates multiplier effects where a single action satisfies multiple scoring conditions simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Speed Versus Value Trade-off
walkthrough.text: High-speed rescue cards let you pick first, giving you the best tiles. But sometimes a slower rescue with a better color match is more valuable. Advanced players evaluate whether going first matters this round. If the island has multiple good options in your colors, speed is less important. If there is exactly one perfect tile, speed is critical. Adapt your draft to the island state.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Scoring Push
walkthrough.text: In rounds four and five, shift from building your engine to maximizing your score. Stop drafting lesson cards. Focus on rescue cards in your family colors to push families to higher tiers. Use treasures to fill any remaining gaps. Count your room coverage and calculate whether you can fill all seven rooms. If not, focus on the rooms that cost you the least to complete. Every point matters in the final rounds.

summary.title: Grid Optimization Theory
bullets: Plan entire boat layout before placing first tile
Large tiles in middle rooms, small tiles for edges/corners
Mental blueprint reduces wasted space dramatically
Visualize which rooms use which tile sizes

bullets: Spend minimum fish per draft round

Never draft a card you can't afford to use
Unused lesson = wasted fish budget

bullets: Round 1 basket = 4 free rescues (rounds 2-5)
Most efficient cards in the game

Later rounds: baskets lose value (fewer rounds left)

summary.title: Oshax Placement Theory

Merge two groups of 2 into one family of 5 = 0→15 points
Position regular cats to leave Oshax-shaped gaps between groups
Maximize adjacency to focus colors

summary.title: Lesson Card Stacking
bullets: Stack multiple lessons rewarding same behavior
Private lesson + public lesson overlap = multiplier effect
One action satisfying 2-3 scoring conditions = huge value
Draft lessons that overlap with public lessons in play

summary.title: Speed Versus Value Trade-off
bullets: High speed = first pick; but sometimes color match > speed
Multiple good options in your color → speed less important
Exactly one perfect tile → speed critical
Adapt draft to island state each round

summary.title: Late-Game Scoring Push
bullets: Rounds 4-5: stop engine building, maximize score
Focus rescue cards on family colors for tier jumps
Use treasures to fill remaining gaps
Count room coverage — prioritize cheapest rooms to complete

entries.definition: A polyomino-shaped tile in one of five colors representing a rescued cat. Sizes range from one to five squares. Placed on your boat grid during the Rescue Cats phase.
A wild cat tile that counts as any color for family scoring. When adjacent to multiple color groups, you choose which family it joins. Obtained through Oshax cards during the draft.

Your personal grid where rescued cat tiles are placed. Contains seven rooms divided by walls. Unfilled rooms cost negative five points each at game end. Some spaces have rat symbols that cost negative one point if uncovered.
The central board representing the Isle of Cats. Contains fields where cat tiles are placed each round for players to rescue. Also tracks the approaching villain and fish supply.
The game's currency. Used to pay for keeping drafted cards and for interacting with the island. Players start with twenty fish and gain more each round during the Fishing phase.
Determines turn order during the Rescue Cats phase. Higher boot values go first, getting first pick of cat tiles from the island.
A discovery card that determines which color cat you can rescue and your speed priority. Used in pairs to rescue one cat tile. The speed value on the card sets your turn order.
A discovery card revealed during the Read Lessons phase. Creates a shared scoring objective for all players. Examples include points per row with a certain number of cats.
A discovery card kept hidden until game end. Scores based on personal secret objectives. Can be worth ten or more points if conditions are met.
A discovery card that grants access to treasure tiles during the Rare Finds phase. Treasures are small tiles that fill gaps on your boat.
A discovery card that lets you claim an Oshax wild cat tile. Oshax count as any color for family scoring and are extremely valuable.
A card effect that persists between rounds. Lets you rescue a cat without needing a rescue card pair. Extremely valuable when drafted early in the game.

A group of three or more orthogonally adjacent same-colored cat tiles on your boat. Scores based on size: three cats equal eight points, four equal eleven, five equal fifteen, six or more equal twenty.
One of seven areas on your boat divided by interior walls. Each room must be completely filled with tiles to avoid a negative five-point penalty at game end.
A symbol printed on certain spaces of your boat grid. Each uncovered rat costs negative one point at game end. Cover them with tiles to avoid the penalty.
A small tile of one to two squares used to fill gaps on your boat. Does not count as part of a cat family. Helps cover room space and rat symbols.
A special treasure tile earned through specific cards. Worth victory points and fills spaces on your boat. More valuable than common treasures.
The villain approaching the Isle of Cats. Tracked on the day board. Creates narrative urgency for the five-day time limit. Not mechanically active but sets the theme.
A geometric shape made of connected squares, similar to Tetris pieces. Cat tiles come in various polyomino shapes from one to five squares. The core spatial puzzle of the game.
The general term for all cards in the draft deck. Includes rescue cards, lesson cards, treasure cards, and Oshax cards. One hundred and fifty cards total.
Phase one of each round. All players gain fish tokens based on the current day. Fish are the currency for keeping drafted cards.
Phase two of each round. Deal seven cards, draft two at a time, pass remaining. Pay fish costs for each card kept or discard it.
Phase three of each round. Players reveal any public lesson cards drafted this round. Creates shared scoring objectives.
Phase four of each round. Players play rescue card pairs to claim cat tiles from the island and place them on their boats.
Phase five of each round. Players with treasure cards claim treasure tiles to fill gaps on their boats.
A simplified variant that removes card drafting and uses a basic draw-and-play mechanism. Suitable for younger players and families.
A dedicated single-player mode where you play against a simulated opponent. Uses a modified card mechanism instead of drafting.
